Be it so; go back: the virtue of your name
Is not here passable.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
units.text:
I tell thee, fellow,
The general is my lover: I have been
The book of his good acts, whence men have read
His name unparallel'd, haply amplified;
For I have ever verified my friends,
Of whom he's chief, with all the size that verity
Would without lapsing suffer: nay, sometimes,
Like to a bowl upon a subtle ground,
I have tumbled past the throw; and in his praise
Have almost stamp'd the leasing: therefore, fellow,
I must have leave to pass.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text:
Faith, sir, if you had told as many lies in his
behalf as you have uttered words in your own, you
should not pass here; no, though it were as virtuous
to lie as to live chastely. Therefore, go back.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
units.text:
Prithee, fellow, remember my name is Menenius,
always factionary on the party of your general.

units.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
units.text:
Howsoever you have been his liar, as you say you
have, I am one that, telling true under him, must
say, you cannot pass. Therefore, go back.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
units.text:
Has he dined, canst thou tell? for I would not
speak with him till after dinner.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text: You are a Roman, are you?

units.speaker: MENENIUS
units.text: I am, as thy general is.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text:
Then you should hate Rome, as he does. Can you,
when you have pushed out your gates the very
defender of them, and, in a violent popular
ignorance, given your enemy your shield, think to
front his revenges with the easy groans of old
women, the virginal palms of your daughters, or with
the palsied intercession of such a decayed dotant as
you seem to be? Can you think to blow out the
intended fire your city is ready to flame in, with
such weak breath as this? No, you are deceived;
therefore, back to Rome, and prepare for your
execution: you are condemned, our general has sworn
you out of reprieve and pardon.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
units.text:
Sirrah, if thy captain knew I were here, he would
use me with estimation.

units.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
units.text: Come, my captain knows you not.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
units.text: I mean, thy general.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text:
My general cares not for you. Back, I say, go; lest
I let forth your half-pint of blood; back,—that's
the utmost of your having: back.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
units.text: Nay, but, fellow, fellow,—
Enter CORIOLANUS and AUFIDIUS

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text: What's the matter?

units.speaker: MENENIUS
units.text:
Now, you companion, I'll say an errand for you:
You shall know now that I am in estimation; you shall
perceive that a Jack guardant cannot office me from
my son Coriolanus: guess, but by my entertainment
with him, if thou standest not i' the state of
hanging, or of some death more long in
spectatorship, and crueller in suffering; behold now
presently, and swoon for what's to come upon thee.
[To CORIOLANUS]
The glorious gods sit in hourly synod
about thy particular prosperity and love thee no worse
than thy old father Menenius does. O my son, my son!
Thou art preparing fire for us. Look thee, here's water
to quench it. I was hardly moved to come to thee but,
being assured none but myself could move thee, I have
been blown out of gates with sighs and conjure
thee to pardon Rome and they petitionary countrymen.
The good gods assuage thy wrath and turn the dregs
of it upon this varlet here—
this, who like a block hath denied my access to thee.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text: Away!

units.speaker: MENENIUS
units.text: How! away!

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text:
Wife, mother, child, I know not. My affairs
Are servanted to others: though I owe
My revenge properly, my remission lies
In Volscian breasts. That we have been familiar,
Ingrate forgetfulness shall poison, rather
Than pity note how much. Therefore, be gone.
Mine ears against your suits are stronger than
Your gates against my force. Yet, for I loved thee,
Take this along; I writ it for thy sake
And would have rent it. Another word, Menenius,
I will not hear thee speak. This man, Aufidius,
Was my beloved in Rome: yet thou behold'st!

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text: You keep a constant temper.
Exeunt CORIOLANUS and AUFIDIUS

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text: Now, sir, is your name Menenius?

units.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
units.text:
'Tis a spell, you see, of much power: you know the
way home again.

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text:
Do you hear how we are shent for keeping your
greatness back?

units.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
units.text: What cause, do you think, I have to swoon?

units.speaker: MENENIUS
units.text:
I neither care for the world nor your general: for
such things as you, I can scarce think there's any,
ye're so slight. He that hath a will to die by
himself fears it not from another: let your general
do his worst. For you, be that you are, long; and
your misery increase with your age! I say to you,
as I was said to, Away!
Exit

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text: A noble fellow, I warrant him.

units.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
units.text:
The worthy fellow is our general: he's the rock, the
oak not to be wind-shaken.
Exeunt
Enter CORIOLANUS, AUFIDIUS, and others

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text:
We will before the walls of Rome tomorrow
Set down our host. My partner in this action,
You must report to the Volscian lords, how plainly
I have borne this business.

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text:
Only their ends
You have respected; stopp'd your ears against
The general suit of Rome; never admitted
A private whisper, no, not with such friends
That thought them sure of you.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text:
This last old man,
Whom with a crack'd heart I have sent to Rome,
Loved me above the measure of a father;
Nay, godded me, indeed. Their latest refuge
Was to send him; for whose old love I have,
Though I show'd sourly to him, once more offer'd
The first conditions, which they did refuse
And cannot now accept; to grace him only
That thought he could do more, a very little
I have yielded to: fresh embassies and suits,
Nor from the state nor private friends, hereafter
Will I lend ear to. Ha! what shout is this?
Shall I be tempted to infringe my vow
In the same time 'tis made? I will not.
Enter in mourning habits, VIRGILIA, VOLUMNIA, leading young MARCIUS, VALERIA, and Attendants

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text:
My wife comes foremost; then the honour'd mould
Wherein this trunk was framed, and in her hand
The grandchild to her blood. But, out, affection!
All bond and privilege of nature, break!
Let it be virtuous to be obstinate.
What is that curt'sy worth? or those doves' eyes,
Which can make gods forsworn? I melt, and am not
Of stronger earth than others. My mother bows;
As if Olympus to a molehill should
In supplication nod: and my young boy
Hath an aspect of intercession, which
Great nature cries 'Deny not.' let the Volsces
Plough Rome and harrow Italy: I'll never
Be such a gosling to obey instinct, but stand,
As if a man were author of himself
And knew no other kin.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
units.text: My lord and husband!

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text: These eyes are not the same I wore in Rome.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
units.text:
The sorrow that delivers us thus changed
Makes you think so.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text:
Like a dull actor now,
I have forgot my part, and I am out,
Even to a full disgrace. Best of my flesh,
Forgive my tyranny; but do not say
For that 'Forgive our Romans.' O, a kiss
Long as my exile, sweet as my revenge!
Now, by the jealous queen of heaven, that kiss
I carried from thee, dear; and my true lip
Hath virgin'd it e'er since. You gods! I prate,
And the most noble mother of the world
Leave unsaluted: sink, my knee, i' the earth;

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
units.text:
O, stand up blest!
Whilst, with no softer cushion than the flint,
I kneel before thee; and unproperly
Show duty, as mistaken all this while
Between the child and parent.
Kneels

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text:
What is this?
Your knees to me? to your corrected son?
Then let the pebbles on the hungry beach
Fillip the stars; then let the mutinous winds
Strike the proud cedars 'gainst the fiery sun;
Murdering impossibility, to make
What cannot be, slight work.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
units.text:
Thou art my warrior;
I holp to frame thee. Do you know this lady?

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text:
The noble sister of Publicola,
The moon of Rome, chaste as the icicle
That's curdied by the frost from purest snow
And hangs on Dian's temple: dear Valeria!

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
units.text:
This is a poor epitome of yours,
Which by the interpretation of full time
May show like all yourself.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text:
The god of soldiers,
With the consent of supreme Jove, inform
Thy thoughts with nobleness; that thou mayst prove
To shame unvulnerable, and stick i' the wars
Like a great sea-mark, standing every flaw,
And saving those that eye thee!

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
units.text: Your knee, sirrah.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text: That's my brave boy!

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
units.text:
Even he, your wife, this lady, and myself,
Are suitors to you.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text:
I beseech you, peace:
Or, if you'ld ask, remember this before:
The thing I have forsworn to grant may never
Be held by you denials. Do not bid me
Dismiss my soldiers, or capitulate
Again with Rome's mechanics: tell me not
Wherein I seem unnatural: desire not
To ally my rages and revenges with
Your colder reasons.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
CORIOLANUS
units.text:
Aufidius, and you Volsces, mark; for we'll
Hear nought from Rome in private. Your request?

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
units.text:
Should we be silent and not speak, our raiment
And state of bodies would bewray what life
We have led since thy exile. Think with thyself
How more unfortunate than all living women
Are we come hither: since that thy sight,
which should
Make our eyes flow with joy, hearts dance
with comforts,
Constrains them weep and shake with fear and sorrow;
Making the mother, wife and child to see
The son, the husband and the father tearing
His country's bowels out. And to poor we
Thine enmity's most capital: thou barr'st us
Our prayers to the gods, which is a comfort
That all but we enjoy; for how can we,
Alas, how can we for our country pray.
Whereto we are bound, together with thy victory,
Whereto we are bound? alack, or we must lose
The country, our dear nurse, or else thy person,
Our comfort in the country. We must find
An evident calamity, though we had
Our wish, which side should win: for either thou
Must, as a foreign recreant, be led
With manacles thorough our streets, or else
triumphantly tread on thy country's ruin,
And bear the palm for having bravely shed
Thy wife and children's blood. For myself, son,
I purpose not to wait on fortune till
These wars determine: if I cannot persuade thee
Rather to show a noble grace to both parts
Than seek the end of one, thou shalt no sooner
March to assault thy country than to tread—
Trust to't, thou shalt not— on thy mother's womb,
That brought thee to this world.

units.speaker: VIRGILIA
units.text:
Ay, and mine,
That brought you forth this boy, to keep your name
Living to time.

units.speaker: YOUNG MARCIUS
units.text:
A' shall not tread on me;
I'll run away till I am bigger, but then I'll fight.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text:
Not of a woman's tenderness to be,
Requires nor child nor woman's face to see.
I have sat too long.

units.speaker: VOLUMNIA
units.text:
Nay, go not from us thus.
If it were so that our request did tend
To save the Romans, thereby to destroy
The Volsces whom you serve, you might condemn us,
As poisonous of your honour: no; our suit
Is that you reconcile them: while the Volsces
May say 'This mercy we have show'd;' the Romans,
'This we received;' and each in either side
Give the all-hail to thee and cry 'Be blest
For making up this peace!' Thou know'st, great son,
The end of war's uncertain, but this certain,
That, if thou conquer Rome, the benefit
Which thou shalt thereby reap is such a name,
Whose repetition will be dogg'd with curses;
Whose chronicle thus writ: 'The man was noble,
But with his last attempt he wiped it out;
Destroy'd his country, and his name remains
To the ensuing age abhorr'd.' Speak to me, son:
Thou hast affected the fine strains of honour,
To imitate the graces of the gods;
To tear with thunder the wide cheeks o' the air,
And yet to charge thy sulphur with a bolt
That should but rive an oak. Why dost not speak?
Think'st thou it honourable for a noble man
Still to remember wrongs? Daughter, speak you:
He cares not for your weeping. Speak thou, boy:
Perhaps thy childishness will move him more
Than can our reasons. There's no man in the world
More bound to 's mother; yet here he lets me prate
Like one i' the stocks. Thou hast never in thy life
Show'd thy dear mother any courtesy,
When she, poor hen, fond of no second brood,
Has cluck'd thee to the wars and safely home,
Loaden with honour. Say my request's unjust,
And spurn me back: but if it be not so,
Thou art not honest; and the gods will plague thee,
That thou restrain'st from me the duty which
To a mother's part belongs. He turns away:
Down, ladies; let us shame him with our knees.
To his surname Coriolanus 'longs more pride
Than pity to our prayers. Down: an end;
This is the last: so we will home to Rome,
And die among our neighbours. Nay, behold 's:
This boy, that cannot tell what he would have
But kneels and holds up bands for fellowship,
Does reason our petition with more strength
Than thou hast to deny 't. Come, let us go:
This fellow had a Volscian to his mother;
His wife is in Corioli and his child
Like him by chance. Yet give us our dispatch:
I am hush'd until our city be a-fire,
And then I'll speak a little.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text:
O mother, mother!
What have you done? Behold, the heavens do ope,
The gods look down, and this unnatural scene
They laugh at. O my mother, mother! O!
You have won a happy victory to Rome;
But, for your son,— believe it, O, believe it,
Most dangerously you have with him prevail'd,
If not most mortal to him. But, let it come.
Aufidius, though I cannot make true wars,
I'll frame convenient peace. Now, good Aufidius,
Were you in my stead, would you have heard
A mother less? or granted less, Aufidius?

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text: I was moved withal.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text:
I dare be sworn you were:
And, sir, it is no little thing to make
Mine eyes to sweat compassion. But, good sir,
What peace you'll make, advise me: for my part,
I'll not to Rome, I'll back with you; and pray you,
Stand to me in this cause. O mother! wife!

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text:
[Aside] I am glad thou hast set thy mercy and
thy honour
At difference in thee: out of that I'll work
Myself a former fortune.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text:
Ay, by and by;
But we will drink together; and you shall bear
A better witness back than words, which we,
On like conditions, will have counter-seal'd.
Come, enter with us. Ladies, you deserve
To have a temple built you: all the swords
In Italy, and her confederate arms,
Could not have made this peace.
Exeunt
Enter MENENIUS and SICINIUS

units.speaker: MENENIUS
units.text:
See you yond coign o' the Capitol, yond
corner-stone?

units.speaker: SICINIUS
units.text: Why, what of that?

units.speaker: MENENIUS
units.text:
If it be possible for you to displace it with your
little finger, there is some hope the ladies of
Rome, especially his mother, may prevail with him.
But I say there is no hope in't: our throats are
sentenced and stay upon execution.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
units.text:
Is't possible that so short a time can alter the
condition of a man!

units.speaker: MENENIUS
units.text:
There is differency between a grub and a butterfly;
yet your butterfly was a grub. This Marcius is grown
from man to dragon: he has wings; he's more than a
creeping thing.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
units.text: He loved his mother dearly.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
units.text:
So did he me: and he no more remembers his mother
now than an eight-year-old horse. The tartness
of his face sours ripe grapes: when he walks, he
moves like an engine, and the ground shrinks before
his treading: he is able to pierce a corslet with
his eye; talks like a knell, and his hum is a
battery. He sits in his state, as a thing made for
Alexander. What he bids be done is finished with
his bidding. He wants nothing of a god but eternity
and a heaven to throne in.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
units.text: Yes, mercy, if you report him truly.

units.speaker: MENENIUS
units.text:
I paint him in the character. Mark what mercy his
mother shall bring from him: there is no more mercy
in him than there is milk in a male tiger; that
shall our poor city find: and all this is long of
you.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
units.text: The gods be good unto us!

units.speaker: MENENIUS
units.text:
No, in such a case the gods will not be good unto
us. When we banished him, we respected not them;
and, he returning to break our necks, they respect not
us.
Enter a Messenger

units.speaker: MESSENGER
units.text:
Sir, if you'ld save your life, fly to your house:
The plebeians have got your fellow-tribune
And hale him up and down, all swearing, if
The Roman ladies bring not comfort home,
They'll give him death by inches.
Enter a second Messenger

units.speaker: SICINIUS
units.text: What's the news?

units.speaker: SECOND MESSENGER
units.text:
Good news, good news; the ladies have prevail'd,
The Volscians are dislodged, and Marcius gone:
A merrier day did never yet greet Rome,
No, not the expulsion of the Tarquins.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
units.text:
Friend,
Art thou certain this is true? is it most certain?

units.speaker: SECOND MESSENGER
units.text:
As certain as I know the sun is fire:
Where have you lurk'd, that you make doubt of it?
Ne'er through an arch so hurried the blown tide,
As the recomforted through the gates. Why, hark you!
Trumpets; hautboys; drums beat; all together

The trumpets, sackbuts, psalteries and fifes,
Tabours and cymbals and the shouting Romans,
Make the sun dance. Hark you!
A shout within

units.speaker: MENENIUS
units.text:
This is good news:
I will go meet the ladies. This Volumnia
Is worth of consuls, senators, patricians,
A city full; of tribunes, such as you,
A sea and land full. You have pray'd well to-day:
This morning for ten thousand of your throats
I'd not have given a doit. Hark, how they joy!
Music still, with shouts

units.speaker: SICINIUS
units.text:
First, the gods bless you for your tidings; next,
Accept my thankfulness.

units.speaker: SECOND MESSENGER
units.text:
Sir, we have all
Great cause to give great thanks.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
units.text: They are near the city?

units.speaker: SECOND MESSENGER
units.text: Almost at point to enter.

units.speaker: SICINIUS
units.text:
We will meet them,
And help the joy.
Exeunt
Enter two Senators with VOLUMNIA, VIRGILIA, VALERIA, & c. passing over the stage, followed by Patricians and others

units.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
units.text:
Behold our patroness, the life of Rome!
Call all your tribes together, praise the gods,
And make triumphant fires; strew flowers before them:
Unshout the noise that banish'd Marcius,
Repeal him with the welcome of his mother;
Cry 'Welcome, ladies, welcome!'

units.speaker: ALL
units.text: Welcome, ladies, Welcome!
A flourish with drums and trumpets. Exeunt
Enter TULLUS AUFIDIUS, with Attendants

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text:
Go tell the lords o' the city I am here:
Deliver them this paper: having read it,
Bid them repair to the market place; where I,
Even in theirs and in the commons' ears,
Will vouch the truth of it. Him I accuse
The city ports by this hath enter'd a nd
Intends to appear before the people, hoping
To purge himself with words: dispatch.
Exeunt Attendants
Enter three or four Conspirators of AUFIDIUS' faction

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text: Most welcome!

units.speaker: FIRST CONSPIRATOR
units.text: How is it with our general?

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text:
Even so
As with a man by his own alms empoison'd,
And with his charity slain.

units.speaker: SECOND CONSPIRATOR
units.text:
Most noble sir,
If you do hold the same intent wherein
You wish'd us parties, we'll deliver you
Of your great danger.

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text:
Sir, I cannot tell:
We must proceed as we do find the people.

units.speaker: THIRD CONSPIRATOR
units.text:
The people will remain uncertain whilst
'Twixt you there's difference; but the fall of either
Makes the survivor heir of all.

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text:
I know it;
And my pretext to strike at him admits
A good construction. I raised him, and I pawn'd
Mine honour for his truth: who being so heighten'd,
He water'd his new plants with dews of flattery,
Seducing so my friends; and, to this end,
He bow'd his nature, never known before
But to be rough, unswayable and free.

units.speaker: THIRD CONSPIRATOR
units.text:
Sir, his stoutness
When he did stand for consul, which he lost
By lack of stooping,—

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text:
That I would have spoke of:
Being banish'd for't, he came unto my hearth;
Presented to my knife his throat: I took him;
Made him joint-servant with me; gave him way
In all his own desires; nay, let him choose
Out of my files, his projects to accomplish,
My best and freshest men; served his designments
In mine own person; holp to reap the fame
Which he did end all his; and took some pride
To do myself this wrong: till, at the last,
I seem'd his follower, not partner, and
He waged me with his countenance, as if
I had been mercenary.

units.speaker: FIRST CONSPIRATOR
units.text:
So he did, my lord:
The army marvell'd at it, and, in the last,
When he had carried Rome and that we look'd
For no less spoil than glory,—

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text:
There was it:
For which my sinews shall be stretch'd upon him.
At a few drops of women's rheum, which are
As cheap as lies, he sold the blood and labour
Of our great action: therefore shall he die,
And I'll renew me in his fall. But, hark!
Drums and trumpets sound, with great shouts of the People

units.speaker: FIRST CONSPIRATOR
units.text:
Your native town you enter'd like a post,
And had no welcomes home: but he returns,
Splitting the air with noise.

units.speaker: SECOND CONSPIRATOR
units.text:
And patient fools,
Whose children he hath slain, their base throats tear
With giving him glory.

units.speaker: THIRD CONSPIRATOR
units.text:
Therefore, at your vantage,
Ere he express himself, or move the people
With what he would say, let him feel your sword,
Which we will second. When he lies along,
After your way his tale pronounced shall bury
His reasons with his body.

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text:
Say no more:
Here come the lords.
Enter the Lords of the city

units.speaker: ALL THE LORDS
units.text: You are most welcome home.

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text:
I have not deserved it.
But, worthy lords, have you with heed perused
What I have written to you?

units.speaker: LORDS
units.text: We have.

units.speaker: FIRST LORD
units.text:
And grieve to hear't.
What faults he made before the last, I think
Might have found easy fines: but there to end
Where he was to begin and give away
The benefit of our levies, answering us
With our own charge, making a treaty where
There was a yielding,— this admits no excuse.

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text: He approaches: you shall hear him.
Enter CORIOLANUS, marching with drum and colours; commoners being with him

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text:
Hail, lords! I am return'd your soldier,
No more infected with my country's love
Than when I parted hence, but still subsisting
Under your great command. You are to know
That prosperously I have attempted and
With bloody passage led your wars even to
The gates of Rome. Our spoils we have brought home
Do more than counterpoise a full third part
The charges of the action. We have made peace
With no less honour to the Antiates
Than shame to the Romans: and we here deliver,
Subscribed by the consuls and patricians,
Together with the seal o' the senate, what
We have compounded on.

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
CORIOLANUS
units.text: Traitor! how now!

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text: Ay, traitor, Marcius!

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text: Marcius!

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text:
Ay, Marcius, Caius Marcius: dost thou think
I'll grace thee with that robbery, thy stol'n name
Coriolanus in Corioli?
You lords and heads o' the state, perfidiously
He has betray'd your business, and given up,
For certain drops of salt, your city Rome,
I say 'your city,' to his wife and mother;
Breaking his oath and resolution like
A twist of rotten silk, never admitting
Counsel o' the war, but at his nurse's tears
He whined and roar'd away your victory,
That pages blush'd at him and men of heart
Look'd wondering each at other.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text: Hear'st thou, Mars?

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text: Name not the god, thou boy of tears!

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text: Ha!

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text: No more.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text:
Measureless liar, thou hast made my heart
Too great for what contains it. Boy! O slave!
Pardon me, lords, 'tis the first time that ever
I was forced to scold. Your judgments, my grave lords,
Must give this cur the lie: and his own notion—
Who wears my stripes impress'd upon him; that
Must bear my beating to his grave— shall join
To thrust the lie unto him.

units.speaker: FIRST LORD
units.text: Peace, both, and hear me speak.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text:
Cut me to pieces, Volsces; men and lads,
Stain all your edges on me. Boy! false hound!
If you have writ your annals true, 'tis there,
That, like an eagle in a dove-cote, I
Flutter'd your Volscians in Corioli:
Alone I did it. Boy!

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text:
Why, noble lords,
Will you be put in mind of his blind fortune,
Which was your shame, by this unholy braggart,
'Fore your own eyes and ears?

units.speaker: ALL CONSPIRATORS
units.text: Let him die for't.

units.speaker: ALL THE PEOPLE
units.text:
'Tear him to pieces.' 'Do it presently.' 'He kill'd
my son.' 'My daughter.' 'He killed my cousin
Marcus.' 'He killed my father.'

units.speaker: SECOND LORD
units.text:
Peace, ho! no outrage: peace!
The man is noble and his fame folds-in
This orb o' the earth. His last offences to us
Shall have judicious hearing. Stand, Aufidius,
And trouble not the peace.

units.speaker: CORIOLANUS
units.text:
O that I had him,
With six Aufidiuses, or more, his tribe,
To use my lawful sword!

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text: Insolent villain!

units.speaker: ALL CONSPIRATORS
units.text: Kill, kill, kill, kill, kill him!
The Conspirators draw, and kill CORIOLANUS: AUFIDIUS stands on his body

units.speaker: LORDS
units.text: Hold, hold, hold, hold!

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text: My noble masters, hear me speak.

units.speaker: FIRST LORD
units.text: O Tullus,—

units.speaker: SECOND LORD
units.text: Thou hast done a deed whereat valour will weep.

units.speaker: THIRD LORD
units.text:
Tread not upon him. Masters all, be quiet;
Put up your swords.

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text:
My lords, when you shall know— as in this rage,
Provoked by him, you cannot— the great danger
Which this man's life did owe you, you'll rejoice
That he is thus cut off. Please it your honours
To call me to your senate, I'll deliver
Myself your loyal servant, or endure
Your heaviest censure.

units.speaker: FIRST LORD
units.text:
Bear from hence his body;
And mourn you for him: let him be regarded
As the most noble corse that ever herald
Did follow to his urn.

units.speaker: SECOND LORD
units.text:
His own impatience
Takes from Aufidius a great part of blame.
Let's make the best of it.

units.speaker: AUFIDIUS
units.text:
My rage is gone;
And I am struck with sorrow. Take him up.
Help, three o' the chiefest soldiers; I'll be one.
Beat thou the drum, that it speak mournfully:
Trail your steel pikes. Though in this city he
Hath widow'd and unchilded many a one,
Which to this hour bewail the injury,
Yet he shall have a noble memory. Assist.
Exeunt, bearing the body of CORIOLANUS. A dead march sounded

sentences.text: Enter a company of mutinous Citizens, with staves, clubs, and other weapons

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: Before we proceed any further, hear me speak.

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: Speak, speak.

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: Speak, speak.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: You are all resolved rather to die than to famish?

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: Resolved. resolved.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
First, you know Caius Marcius is chief enemy to the
people.

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: We know't, we know't.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Let us kill him, and we'll have corn at our own price.
Is't a verdict?

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Let us kill him, and we'll have corn at our own price.
Is't a verdict?

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: No more talking on't; let it be done: away, away!

sentences.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
sentences.text: One word, good citizens.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
We are accounted poor citizens, the patricians good.
What authority surfeits on would relieve us: if they
would yield us but the superfluity, while it were
wholesome, we might guess they relieved us humanely;
but they think we are too dear: the leanness that
afflicts us, the object of our misery, is as an
inventory to particularise their abundance; our
sufferance is a gain to them. Let us revenge this with
our pikes, ere we become rakes: for the gods know I
speak this in hunger for bread, not in thirst for
revenge.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
We are accounted poor citizens, the patricians good.
What authority surfeits on would relieve us: if they
would yield us but the superfluity, while it were
wholesome, we might guess they relieved us humanely;
but they think we are too dear: the leanness that
afflicts us, the object of our misery, is as an
inventory to particularise their abundance; our
sufferance is a gain to them. Let us revenge this with
our pikes, ere we become rakes: for the gods know I
speak this in hunger for bread, not in thirst for
revenge.

sufferance is a gain to them. Let us revenge this with
our pikes, ere we become rakes: for the gods know I
speak this in hunger for bread, not in thirst for
revenge.

sentences.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
sentences.text: Would you proceed especially against Caius Marcius?

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: Against him first: he's a very dog to the commonalty.

sentences.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
sentences.text: Consider you what services he has done for his country?

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Very well; and could be content to give him good
report fort, but that he pays himself with being proud.

sentences.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
sentences.text: Nay, but speak not maliciously.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
I say unto you, what he hath done famously, he did
it to that end: though soft-conscienced men can be
content to say it was for his country he did it to
please his mother and to be partly proud; which he
is, even till the altitude of his virtue.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:

it to that end: though soft-conscienced men can be
content to say it was for his country he did it to
please his mother and to be partly proud; which he
is, even till the altitude of his virtue.

sentences.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
sentences.text:
What he cannot help in his nature, you account a
vice in him. You must in no way say he is covetous.

sentences.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
sentences.text:
What he cannot help in his nature, you account a
vice in him. You must in no way say he is covetous.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
If I must not, I need not be barren of accusations;
he hath faults, with surplus, to tire in repetition.
Shouts within

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
What shouts are these? The other side o' the city
is risen: why stay we prating here? to the Capitol!

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
What shouts are these? The other side o' the city
is risen: why stay we prating here? to the Capitol!

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
What shouts are these? The other side o' the city
is risen: why stay we prating here? to the Capitol!

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
What shouts are these? The other side o' the city
is risen: why stay we prating here? to the Capitol!

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: Come, come.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: Soft! who comes here?

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: Soft! who comes here?
Enter MENENIUS AGRIPPA

sentences.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Worthy Menenius Agrippa; one that hath always loved
the people.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: He's one honest enough: would all the rest were so!

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
What work's, my countrymen, in hand? where go you
With bats and clubs? The matter? speak, I pray you.

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
What work's, my countrymen, in hand? where go you
With bats and clubs? The matter? speak, I pray you.

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
What work's, my countrymen, in hand? where go you
With bats and clubs? The matter? speak, I pray you.

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
What work's, my countrymen, in hand? where go you
With bats and clubs? The matter? speak, I pray you.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Our business is not unknown to the senate; they have
had inkling this fortnight what we intend to do,
which now we'll show 'em in deeds. They say poor
suitors have strong breaths: they shall know we
have strong arms too.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Our business is not unknown to the senate; they have
had inkling this fortnight what we intend to do,
which now we'll show 'em in deeds. They say poor
suitors have strong breaths: they shall know we
have strong arms too.

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
Why, masters, my good friends, mine honest neighbours,
Will you undo yourselves?

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
Why, masters, my good friends, mine honest neighbours,
Will you undo yourselves?

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: We cannot, sir, we are undone already.

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
I tell you, friends, most charitable care
Have the patricians of you. For your wants,
Your suffering in this dearth, you may as well
Strike at the heaven with your staves as lift them
Against the Roman state, whose course will on
The way it takes, cracking ten thousand curbs
Of more strong link asunder than can ever
Appear in your impediment. For the dearth,
The gods, not the patricians, make it, and
Your knees to them, not arms, must help. Alack,
You are transported by calamity
Thither where more attends you, and you slander
The helms o' the state, who care for you like fathers,
When you curse them as enemies.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Care for us! True, indeed! They ne'er cared for us
yet: suffer us to famish, and their store-houses
crammed with grain; make edicts for usury, to
support usurers; repeal daily any wholesome act
established against the rich, and provide more
piercing statutes daily, to chain up and restrain
the poor. If the wars eat us not up, they will; and
there's all the love they bear us.

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
Either you must
Confess yourselves wondrous malicious,
Or be accused of folly. I shall tell you
A pretty tale: it may be you have heard it;
But, since it serves my purpose, I will venture
To stale 't a little more.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Well, I'll hear it, sir: yet you must not think to
fob off our disgrace with a tale: but, an 't please
you, deliver.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Well, I'll hear it, sir: yet you must not think to
fob off our disgrace with a tale: but, an 't please

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
There was a time when all the body's members
Rebell'd against the belly, thus accused it:
That only like a gulf it did remain
I' the midst o' the body, idle and unactive,
Still cupboarding the viand, never bearing
Like labour with the rest, where the other instruments
Did see and hear, devise, instruct, walk, feel,
And, mutually participate, did minister
Unto the appetite and affection common
Of the whole body. The belly answer'd—

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:

That only like a gulf it did remain

That only like a gulf it did remain

Did see and hear, devise, instruct, walk, feel,
And, mutually participate, did minister
Unto the appetite and affection common
Of the whole body. The belly answer'd—

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: Well, sir, what answer made the belly?

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: Well, sir, what answer made the belly?

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
Sir, I shall tell you. With a kind of smile,
Which ne'er came from the lungs, but even thus—
For, look you, I may make the belly smile
As well as speak—it tauntingly replied
To the discontented members, the mutinous parts
That envied his receipt; even so most fitly
As you malign our senators for that
They are not such as you.

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
Sir, I shall tell you. With a kind of smile,

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Your belly's answer? What!
The kingly-crowned head, the vigilant eye,
The counsellor heart, the arm our soldier,
Our steed the leg, the tongue our trumpeter.
With other muniments and petty helps
In this our fabric, if that they—

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
What then?
'Fore me, this fellow speaks! What then? what then?

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
What then?
'Fore me, this fellow speaks! What then? what then?

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
What then?
'Fore me, this fellow speaks! What then? what then?

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
What then?
'Fore me, this fellow speaks! What then? what then?

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
Should by the cormorant belly be restrain'd,
Who is the sink o' the body,—

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text: Well, what then?

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text:
The former agents, if they did complain,
What could the belly answer?

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
I will tell you
If you'll bestow a small—of what you have little—
Patience awhile, you'll hear the belly's answer.

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: Ye're long about it.

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
Note me this, good friend;
Your most grave belly was deliberate,
Not rash like his accusers, and thus answer'd:
'True is it, my incorporate friends,' quoth he,
'That I receive the general food at first,
Which you do live upon; and fit it is,
Because I am the store-house and the shop
Of the whole body: but, if you do remember,
I send it through the rivers of your blood,
Even to the court, the heart, to the seat o' the brain;
And, through the cranks and offices of man,
The strongest nerves and small inferior veins
From me receive that natural competency
Whereby they live: and though that all at once,
You, my good friends,'—this says the belly, mark me,—

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: Ay, sir; well, well.

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
'Though all at once cannot
See what I do deliver out to each,
Yet I can make my audit up, that all
From me do back receive the flour of all,
And leave me but the bran.' What say you to't?

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: It was an answer: how apply you this?

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
The senators of Rome are this good belly,
And you the mutinous members; for examine
Their counsels and their cares, digest things rightly
Touching the weal o' the common, you shall find
No public benefit which you receive
But it proceeds or comes from them to you
And no way from yourselves. What do you think,
You, the great toe of this assembly?

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:

And no way from yourselves. What do you think,

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: I the great toe! why the great toe?

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
For that, being one o' the lowest, basest, poorest,
Of this most wise rebellion, thou go'st foremost:
Thou rascal, that art worst in blood to run,
Lead'st first to win some vantage.
But make you ready your stiff bats and clubs:
Rome and her rats are at the point of battle;
The one side must have bale.

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:

Enter CAIUS MARCIUS

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text: Hail, noble Marcius!

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Thanks. What's the matter, you dissentious rogues,
That, rubbing the poor itch of your opinion,
Make yourselves scabs?

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Thanks. What's the matter, you dissentious rogues,
That, rubbing the poor itch of your opinion,
Make yourselves scabs?

sentences.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
sentences.text: We have ever your good word.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
He that will give good words to thee will flatter
Beneath abhorring. What would you have, you curs,
That like nor peace nor war? the one affrights you,
The other makes you proud. He that trusts to you,
Where he should find you lions, finds you hares;
Where foxes, geese: you are no surer, no,
Than is the coal of fire upon the ice,
Or hailstone in the sun. Your virtue is
To make him worthy whose offence subdues him
And curse that justice did it.
Who deserves greatness
Deserves your hate; and your affections are
A sick man's appetite, who desires most that
Which would increase his evil. He that depends
Upon your favours swims with fins of lead
And hews down oaks with rushes. Hang ye! Trust Ye?
With every minute you do change a mind,
And call him noble that was now your hate,
Him vile that was your garland. What's the matter,
That in these several places of the city
You cry against the noble senate, who,
Under the gods, keep you in awe, which else
Would feed on one another? What's their seeking?

And call him noble that was now your hate,
Him vile that was your garland. What's the matter,
That in these several places of the city
You cry against the noble senate, who,
Under the gods, keep you in awe, which else
Would feed on one another? What's their seeking?

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
He that will give good words to thee will flatter
Beneath abhorring. What would you have, you curs,
That like nor peace nor war? the one affrights you,
The other makes you proud. He that trusts to you,
Where he should find you lions, finds you hares;
Where foxes, geese: you are no surer, no,
Than is the coal of fire upon the ice,
Or hailstone in the sun. Your virtue is
To make him worthy whose offence subdues him
And curse that justice did it.
Who deserves greatness
Deserves your hate; and your affections are
A sick man's appetite, who desires most that
Which would increase his evil. He that depends
Upon your favours swims with fins of lead
And hews down oaks with rushes. Hang ye! Trust Ye?
With every minute you do change a mind,
And call him noble that was now your hate,
Him vile that was your garland. What's the matter,
That in these several places of the city
You cry against the noble senate, who,
Under the gods, keep you in awe, which else
Would feed on one another? What's their seeking?

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
For corn at their own rates; whereof, they say,
The city is well stored.

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
For corn at their own rates; whereof, they say,
The city is well stored.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Hang 'em! They say!
They'll sit by the fire, and presume to know
What's done i' the Capitol; who's like to rise,
Who thrives and who declines; side factions
and give out
Conjectural marriages; making parties strong
And feebling such as stand not in their liking
Below their cobbled shoes. They say there's
grain enough!
Would the nobility lay aside their ruth,
And let me use my sword, I'll make a quarry
With thousands of these quarter'd slaves, as high
As I could pick my lance.

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
Nay, these are almost thoroughly persuaded;
For though abundantly they lack discretion,
Yet are they passing cowardly. But, I beseech you,
What says the other troop?

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:

What says the other troop?

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text:
Nay, these are almost thoroughly persuaded;
For though abundantly they lack discretion,

What says the other troop?

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
They are dissolved: hang 'em!
They said they were an-hungry; sigh'd forth proverbs,
That hunger broke stone walls, that dogs must eat,
That meat was made for mouths, that the gods sent not
Corn for the rich men only: with these shreds
They vented their complainings; which being answer'd,
And a petition granted them, a strange one—
To break the heart of generosity,
And make bold power look pale— they threw their caps
As they would hang them on the horns o' the moon,
Shouting their emulation.

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text: What is granted them?

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Five tribunes to defend their vulgar wisdoms,
Of their own choice: one's Junius Brutus,
Sicinius Velutus, and I know not— 'Sdeath!
The rabble should have first unroof'd the city,
Ere so prevail'd with me: it will in time
Win upon power and throw forth greater themes
For insurrection's arguing.

sentences.speaker: MENENIUS
sentences.text: This is strange.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text: Go, get you home, you fragments!
Enter a Messenger, hastily

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text: Where's Caius Marcius?

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text: Here: what's the matter?

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text: The news is, sir, the Volsces are in arms.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
I am glad on 't: then we shall ha' means to vent
Our musty superfluity. See, our best elders.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
I am glad on 't: then we shall ha' means to vent
Our musty superfluity. See, our best elders.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
I am glad on 't: then we shall ha' means to vent
Our musty superfluity. See, our best elders.
Enter COMINIUS, TITUS LARTIUS, and other Senators; JUNIUS BRUTUS and SICINIUS VELUTUS

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
Marcius, 'tis true that you have lately told us;
The Volsces are in arms.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
They have a leader,
Tullus Aufidius, that will put you to 't.
I sin in envying his nobility,
And were I any thing but what I am,
I would wish me only he.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
They have a leader,
Tullus Aufidius, that will put you to 't.
I sin in envying his nobility,
And were I any thing but what I am,
I would wish me only he.

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text: You have fought together.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Were half to half the world by the ears and he.
Upon my party, I'd revolt to make
Only my wars with him: he is a lion
That I am proud to hunt.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Were half to half the world by the ears and he.
Upon my party, I'd revolt to make
Only my wars with him: he is a lion
That I am proud to hunt.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
Then, worthy Marcius,
Attend upon Cominius to these wars.

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text: It is your former promise.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Sir, it is;
And I am constant. Titus Lartius, thou
Shalt see me once more strike at Tullus' face.
What, art thou stiff? stand'st out?

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Sir, it is;

Shalt see me once more strike at Tullus' face.
What, art thou stiff? stand'st out?

What, art thou stiff? stand'st out?

What, art thou stiff? stand'st out?

sentences.speaker: TITUS
MENENIUS
sentences.text: O, true-bred!

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
Your company to the Capitol; where, I know,
Our greatest friends attend us.

sentences.speaker: TITUS
COMINIUS
sentences.text: Noble Marcius!

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text: [To the Citizens] Hence to your homes; be gone!

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Nay, let them follow:
The Volsces have much corn; take these rats thither
To gnaw their garners. Worshipful mutiners,
Your valour puts well forth: pray, follow.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Nay, let them follow:
The Volsces have much corn; take these rats thither
To gnaw their garners. Worshipful mutiners,
Your valour puts well forth: pray, follow.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Nay, let them follow:
The Volsces have much corn; take these rats thither
To gnaw their garners. Worshipful mutiners,
Your valour puts well forth: pray, follow.
Citizens steal away. Exeunt all but SICINIUS and BRUTUS
Citizens steal away. Exeunt all but SICINIUS and BRUTUS

sentences.speaker: SICINIUS
sentences.text: Was ever man so proud as is this Marcius?

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text: He has no equal.

sentences.speaker: SICINIUS
sentences.text: When we were chosen tribunes for the people,—

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text: Mark'd you his lip and eyes?

sentences.speaker: SICINIUS
sentences.text: Nay. but his taunts.

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text: Being moved, he will not spare to gird the gods.

sentences.speaker: SICINIUS
sentences.text: Be-mock the modest moon.

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text:
The present wars devour him: he is grown
Too proud to be so valiant.

sentences.speaker: SICINIUS
sentences.text:
Such a nature,
Tickled with good success, disdains the shadow
Which he treads on at noon: but I do wonder
His insolence can brook to be commanded
Under Cominius.

sentences.speaker: SICINIUS
sentences.text:
Such a nature,
Tickled with good success, disdains the shadow
Which he treads on at noon: but I do wonder
His insolence can brook to be commanded
Under Cominius.

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text:
Fame, at the which he aims,
In whom already he's well graced, can not
Better be held nor more attain'd than by
A place below the first: for what miscarries
Shall be the general's fault, though he perform
To the utmost of a man, and giddy censure
Will then cry out of Marcius 'O if he
Had borne the business!'

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text:
Fame, at the which he aims,
In whom already he's well graced, can not
Better be held nor more attain'd than by
A place below the first: for what miscarries
Shall be the general's fault, though he perform
To the utmost of a man, and giddy censure
Will then cry out of Marcius 'O if he
Had borne the business!'

sentences.speaker: SICINIUS
sentences.text:
Besides, if things go well,
Opinion that so sticks on Marcius shall
Of his demerits rob Cominius.

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text:
Come:
Half all Cominius' honours are to Marcius.
Though Marcius earned them not, and all his faults
To Marcius shall be honours, though indeed
In aught he merit not.

sentences.speaker: SICINIUS
sentences.text:
Let's hence, and hear
How the dispatch is made, and in what fashion,
More than his singularity, he goes
Upon this present action.

sentences.speaker: BRUTUS
sentences.text: Lets along.
Exeunt
Enter TULLUS AUFIDIUS and certain Senators

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
So, your opinion is, Aufidius,
That they of Rome are entered in our counsels
And know how we proceed.

sentences.speaker: AUFIDIUS
sentences.text:
Is it not yours?
What ever have been thought on in this state,
That could be brought to bodily act ere Rome
Had circumvention? 'Tis not four days gone
Since I heard thence; these are the words: I think
I have the letter here; yes, here it is.

sentences.speaker: AUFIDIUS
sentences.text:

Had circumvention? 'Tis not four days gone
Since I heard thence; these are the words: I think
I have the letter here; yes, here it is.

sentences.speaker: AUFIDIUS
sentences.text:
'They have press'd a power, but it is not known
Whether for east or west: the dearth is great;
The people mutinous; and it is rumour'd,
Cominius, Marcius your old enemy,
Who is of Rome worse hated than of you,
And Titus Lartius, a most valiant Roman,
These three lead on this preparation
Whither 'tis bent: most likely 'tis for you:
Consider of it.'

sentences.text:

And Titus Lartius, a most valiant Roman,

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
Our army's in the field
We never yet made doubt but Rome was ready
To answer us.

sentences.speaker: AUFIDIUS
sentences.text:
Nor did you think it folly
To keep your great pretences veil'd till when
They needs must show themselves; which
in the hatching,
It seem'd, appear'd to Rome. By the discovery
We shall be shorten'd in our aim, which was
To take in many towns ere almost Rome
Should know we were afoot.

sentences.speaker: AUFIDIUS
sentences.text:

in the hatching,

sentences.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
sentences.text:
Noble Aufidius,
Take your commission; hie you to your bands:
Let us alone to guard Corioli:
If they set down before 's, for the remove
Bring your army; but, I think, you'll find
They've not prepared for us.

sentences.speaker: AUFIDIUS
sentences.text:
O, doubt not that;
I speak from certainties. Nay, more,
Some parcels of their power are forth already,
And only hitherward. I leave your honours.
If we and Caius Marcius chance to meet,
'Tis sworn between us we shall ever strike
Till one can do no more.

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: The gods assist you!

sentences.speaker: AUFIDIUS
sentences.text: And keep your honours safe!

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text: Farewell.

sentences.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
sentences.text: Farewell.

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: Farewell.
Exeunt
Enter VOLUMNIA and VIRGILIA. They set them down on two low stools, and sew
Enter VOLUMNIA and VIRGILIA. They set them down on two low stools, and sew

sentences.speaker: VOLUMNIA
sentences.text:
I pray you, daughter, sing; or express yourself in a
more comfortable sort: if my son were my husband, I
should freelier rejoice in that absence wherein he
won honour than in the embracements of his bed where
he would show most love. When yet he was but
tender-bodied and the only son of my womb, when
youth with comeliness plucked all gaze his way, when
for a day of kings' entreaties a mother should not
sell him an hour from her beholding, I, considering
how honour would become such a person—that it was
no better than picture-like to hang by the wall—if
renown made it not stir, was pleased to let him seek
danger where he was like to find fame. To a cruel
war I sent him; from whence he returned, his brows
bound with oak. I tell thee, daughter, I sprang not
more in joy at first hearing he was a man-child
than now in first seeing he had proved himself a man.

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text: But had he died in the business, madam; how then?

sentences.speaker: VOLUMNIA
sentences.text:
Then his good report should have been my son; I
therein would have found issue. Hear me profess
sincerely: had I a dozen sons, each in my love
alike and none less dear than thine and my good
Marcius, I had rather had eleven die nobly for their
country than one voluptuously surfeit out of action.

sentences.speaker: VOLUMNIA
sentences.text:
Then his good report should have been my son; I
therein would have found issue. Hear me profess
sincerely: had I a dozen sons, each in my love
alike and none less dear than thine and my good
Marcius, I had rather had eleven die nobly for their
country than one voluptuously surfeit out of action.
Enter a Gentlewoman

sentences.speaker: GENTLEWOMAN
sentences.text: Madam, the Lady Valeria is come to visit you.

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text: Beseech you, give me leave to retire myself.

sentences.speaker: VOLUMNIA
sentences.text:
Indeed, you shall not.
Methinks I hear hither your husband's drum,
See him pluck Aufidius down by the hair,
As children from a bear, the Volsces shunning him:
Methinks I see him stamp thus, and call thus:
'Come on, you cowards! you were got in fear,
Though you were born in Rome:' his bloody brow
With his mail'd hand then wiping, forth he goes,
Like to a harvest-man that's task'd to mow
Or all or lose his hire.

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text: His bloody brow! O Jupiter, no blood!

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text: His bloody brow! O Jupiter, no blood!

sentences.speaker: VOLUMNIA
sentences.text:
Away, you fool! it more becomes a man
Than gilt his trophy: the breasts of Hecuba,
When she did suckle Hector, look'd not lovelier
Than Hector's forehead when it spit forth blood
At Grecian sword, contemning. Tell Valeria,
We are fit to bid her welcome.

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text: Heavens bless my lord from fell Aufidius!

sentences.speaker: VOLUMNIA
sentences.text:
He'll beat Aufidius 'head below his knee
And tread upon his neck.
Enter VALERIA, with an Usher and Gentlewoman

sentences.speaker: VALERIA
sentences.text: My ladies both, good day to you.

sentences.speaker: VOLUMNIA
sentences.text: Sweet madam.

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text: I am glad to see your ladyship.

sentences.speaker: VALERIA
sentences.text:
How do you both? you are manifest house-keepers.
What are you sewing here? A fine spot, in good
faith. How does your little son?

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text: I thank your ladyship; well, good madam.

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text: I thank your ladyship; well, good madam.

sentences.speaker: VOLUMNIA
sentences.text:
He had rather see the swords, and hear a drum, than
look upon his school-master.

sentences.speaker: VALERIA
sentences.text:
O' my word, the father's son: I'll swear,'tis a
very pretty boy. O' my troth, I looked upon him o'
Wednesday half an hour together: has such a
confirmed countenance. I saw him run after a gilded
butterfly: and when he caught it, he let it go
again; and after it again; and over and over he
comes, and again; catched it again; or whether his
fall enraged him, or how 'twas, he did so set his
teeth and tear it; O, I warrant it, how he mammocked
it!

sentences.speaker: VOLUMNIA
sentences.text: One on 's father's moods.

sentences.speaker: VALERIA
sentences.text: Indeed, la, 'tis a noble child.

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text: A crack, madam.

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text: A crack, madam.

sentences.speaker: VALERIA
sentences.text:
Come, lay aside your stitchery; I must have you play
the idle husewife with me this afternoon.

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text: No, good madam; I will not out of doors.

sentences.speaker: VALERIA
sentences.text: Not out of doors!

sentences.speaker: VOLUMNIA
sentences.text: She shall, she shall.

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text:
Indeed, no, by your patience; I'll not over the
threshold till my lord return from the wars.

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text:
Indeed, no, by your patience; I'll not over the
threshold till my lord return from the wars.

sentences.speaker: VALERIA
sentences.text:
Fie, you confine yourself most unreasonably: come,
you must go visit the good lady that lies in.

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text:
I will wish her speedy strength, and visit her with
my prayers; but I cannot go thither.

sentences.speaker: VOLUMNIA
sentences.text: Why, I pray you?

sentences.speaker: VOLUMNIA
sentences.text: Why, I pray you?

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text: 'Tis not to save labour, nor that I want love.

sentences.speaker: VALERIA
sentences.text:
You would be another Penelope: yet, they say, all
the yarn she spun in Ulysses' absence did but fill
Ithaca full of moths. Come; I would your cambric
were sensible as your finger, that you might leave
pricking it for pity. Come, you shall go with us.

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text: No, good madam, pardon me; indeed, I will not forth.

sentences.speaker: VALERIA
sentences.text:
In truth, la, go with me; and I'll tell you
excellent news of your husband.

sentences.speaker: VALERIA
sentences.text:
In truth, la, go with me; and I'll tell you
excellent news of your husband.

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text: O, good madam, there can be none yet.

sentences.speaker: VALERIA
sentences.text:
Verily, I do not jest with you; there came news from
him last night.

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text: Indeed, madam?

sentences.speaker: VALERIA
sentences.text:
In earnest, it's true; I heard a senator speak it.
Thus it is: the Volsces have an army forth; against
whom Cominius the general is gone, with one part of
our Roman power: your lord and Titus Lartius are set
down before their city Corioli; they nothing doubt
prevailing and to make it brief wars. This is true,
on mine honour; and so, I pray, go with us.

Thus it is: the Volsces have an army forth; against
whom Cominius the general is gone, with one part of
our Roman power: your lord and Titus Lartius are set
down before their city Corioli; they nothing doubt
prevailing and to make it brief wars. This is true,
on mine honour; and so, I pray, go with us.

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text:
Give me excuse, good madam; I will obey you in every
thing hereafter.

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA
sentences.text:
Give me excuse, good madam; I will obey you in every
thing hereafter.

sentences.speaker: VOLUMNIA
sentences.text:
Let her alone, lady: as she is now, she will but
disease our better mirth.

sentences.speaker: VOLUMNIA
sentences.text:
Let her alone, lady: as she is now, she will but
disease our better mirth.

sentences.speaker: VALERIA
sentences.text:
In troth, I think she would. Fare you well, then.
Come, good sweet lady. Prithee, Virgilia, turn thy
solemness out o' door. and go along with us.

No, at a word, madam; indeed, I must not. I wish

sentences.speaker: VIRGILIA

Enter, with drum and colours, MARCIUS, TITUS LARTIUS, Captains and Soldiers. To them a Messenger
Enter, with drum and colours, MARCIUS, TITUS LARTIUS, Captains and Soldiers. To them a Messenger

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text: Yonder comes news. A wager they have met.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text: Yonder comes news. A wager they have met.

sentences.speaker: LARTIUS
sentences.text: My horse to yours, no.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text: 'Tis done.

sentences.speaker: LARTIUS
sentences.text: Agreed.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text: Say, has our general met the enemy?

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text: They lie in view; but have not spoke as yet.

sentences.speaker: LARTIUS
sentences.text: So, the good horse is mine.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text: I'll buy him of you.

sentences.speaker: LARTIUS
sentences.text:
No, I'll nor sell nor give him: lend you him I will
For half a hundred years. Summon the town.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text: How far off lie these armies?

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text: Within this mile and half.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Then shall we hear their 'larum, and they ours.
Now, Mars, I prithee, make us quick in work,
That we with smoking swords may march from hence,
To help our fielded friends! Come, blow thy blast.

They sound a parley. Enter two Senators with others on the walls
They sound a parley. Enter two Senators with others on the walls

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text: Tutus Aufidius, is he within your walls?

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
No, nor a man that fears you less than he,
That's lesser than a little.
Drums afar off

sentences.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
sentences.text:
Hark! our drums
Are bringing forth our youth. We'll break our walls,
Rather than they shall pound us up: our gates,
Which yet seem shut, we, have but pinn'd with rushes;
They'll open of themselves.

sentences.text:
Hark you. far off!
There is Aufidius; list, what work he makes
Amongst your cloven army.

sentences.text: O, they are at it!

sentences.speaker: LARTIUS
sentences.text: Their noise be our instruction. Ladders, ho!

sentences.speaker: LARTIUS
sentences.text: Their noise be our instruction. Ladders, ho!
Enter the army of the Volsces

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
They fear us not, but issue forth their city.
Now put your shields before your hearts, and fight
With hearts more proof than shields. Advance,
brave Titus:
They do disdain us much beyond our thoughts,
Which makes me sweat with wrath. Come on, my fellows:
He that retires I'll take him for a Volsce,
And he shall feel mine edge.

Alarum. The Romans are beat back to their trenches. Re-enter MARCIUS cursing
Alarum. The Romans are beat back to their trenches. Re-enter MARCIUS cursing

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
All the contagion of the south light on you,
You shames of Rome! you herd of— Boils and plagues
Plaster you o'er, that you may be abhorr'd
Further than seen and one infect another
Against the wind a mile! You souls of geese,
That bear the shapes of men, how have you run
From slaves that apes would beat! Pluto and hell!
All hurt behind; backs red, and faces pale
With flight and agued fear! Mend and charge home,
Or, by the fires of heaven, I'll leave the foe
And make my wars on you: l ook to't: come on;
If you'll stand fast, we'll beat them to their wives,
As they us to our trenches followed.

Another alarum. The Volsces fly, and MARCIUS follows them to the gates
Another alarum. The Volsces fly, and MARCIUS follows them to the gates

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
So, now the gates are ope: now prove good seconds:
'Tis for the followers fortune widens them,
Not for the fliers: mark me, and do the like.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: FIRST SOLDIER
sentences.text: Fool-hardiness; not I.

sentences.speaker: SECOND SOLDIER
sentences.text: Nor I.

sentences.speaker: SECOND SOLDIER
sentences.text: Nor I.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SOLDIER
sentences.text: See, they have shut him in.

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: To the pot, I warrant him.
Alarum continues
Re-enter TITUS LARTIUS

sentences.speaker: LARTIUS
sentences.text: What is become of Marcius?

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text: Slain, sir, doubtless.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SOLDIER
sentences.text:
Following the fliers at the very heels,
With them he enters; who, upon the sudden,
Clapp'd to their gates: he is himself alone,
To answer all the city.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SOLDIER
sentences.text:
Following the fliers at the very heels,
With them he enters; who, upon the sudden,

sentences.speaker: LARTIUS
sentences.text:
O noble fellow!
Who sensibly outdares his senseless sword,
And, when it bows, stands up. Thou art left, Marcius:
A carbuncle entire, as big as thou art,
Were not so rich a jewel. Thou wast a soldier
Even to Cato's wish, not fierce and terrible
Only in strokes; but, with thy grim looks and
The thunder-like percussion of thy sounds,
Thou madst thine enemies shake, as if the world
Were feverous and did tremble.

sentences.speaker: FIRST SOLDIER
sentences.text: Look, sir.

sentences.speaker: LARTIUS
sentences.text:
O,'tis Marcius!
Let's fetch him off, or make remain alike.

sentences.speaker: LARTIUS

They fight, and all enter the city
Enter certain Romans, with spoils

sentences.speaker: FIRST ROMAN
sentences.text: This will I carry to Rome.

sentences.speaker: SECOND ROMAN
sentences.text: And I this.

sentences.speaker: THIRD ROMAN
sentences.text: A murrain on't! I took this for silver.

sentences.speaker: THIRD ROMAN
sentences.text: A murrain on't! I took this for silver.
Alarum continues still afar off
Enter MARCIUS and TITUS LARTIUS with a trumpet

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
See here these movers that do prize their hours
At a crack'd drachm! Cushions, leaden spoons,
Irons of a doit, doublets that hangmen would
Bury with those that wore them, these base slaves,
Ere yet the fight be done, pack up: down with them!
And hark, what noise the general makes! To him!
There is the man of my soul's hate, Aufidius,
Piercing our Romans: then, valiant Titus, take
Convenient numbers to make good the city;
Whilst I, with those that have the spirit, will haste
To help Cominius.

MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Sir, praise me not;
My work hath yet not warm'd me: fare you well:
The blood I drop is rather physical
Than dangerous to me: to Aufidius thus
I will appear, and fight.

sentences.speaker: LARTIUS
sentences.text:
Now the fair goddess, Fortune,
Fall deep in love with thee; and her great charms
Misguide thy opposers' swords! Bold gentleman,
Prosperity be thy page!

sentences.speaker: LARTIUS

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Thy friend no less
Than those she placeth highest! So, farewell.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS

sentences.speaker: LARTIUS
sentences.text: Thou worthiest Marcius!
Exit MARCIUS

sentences.speaker: LARTIUS
LARTIUS

sentences.text: Exeunt
Enter COMINIUS, as it were in retire, with soldiers

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
Breathe you, my friends: well fought;
we are come off
Like Romans, neither foolish in our stands,
Nor cowardly in retire: believe me, sirs,
We shall be charged again. Whiles we have struck,
By interims and conveying gusts we have heard
The charges of our friends. Ye Roman gods!
Lead their successes as we wish our own,
That both our powers, with smiling
fronts encountering,
May give you thankful sacrifice.

We shall be charged again. Whiles we have struck,
By interims and conveying gusts we have heard
The charges of our friends. Ye Roman gods!
Lead their successes as we wish our own,
That both our powers, with smiling
fronts encountering,
May give you thankful sacrifice.
Enter a Messenger

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text: Thy news?

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
The citizens of Corioli have issued,
And given to Lartius and to Marcius battle:
I saw our party to their trenches driven,
And then I came away.

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
The citizens of Corioli have issued,
And given to Lartius and to Marcius battle:
I saw our party to their trenches driven,
And then I came away.

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
Though thou speak'st truth,
Methinks thou speak'st not well.
How long is't since?

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
Though thou speak'st truth,
Methinks thou speak'st not well.
How long is't since?

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text: Above an hour, my lord.

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
'Tis not a mile; briefly we heard their drums:
How couldst thou in a mile confound an hour,
And bring thy news so late?

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
'Tis not a mile; briefly we heard their drums:
How couldst thou in a mile confound an hour,
And bring thy news so late?

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
Spies of the Volsces
Held me in chase, that I was forced to wheel
Three or four miles about, else had I, sir,
Half an hour since brought my report.

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
Spies of the Volsces
Held me in chase, that I was forced to wheel
Three or four miles about, else had I, sir,
Half an hour since brought my report.

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
Who's yonder,
That does appear as he were flay'd? O gods
He has the stamp of Marcius; and I have
Before-time seen him thus.

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
Who's yonder,
That does appear as he were flay'd? O gods
He has the stamp of Marcius; and I have
Before-time seen him thus.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text: [Within] Come I too late?

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
The shepherd knows not thunder from a tabour
More than I know the sound of Marcius' tongue
From every meaner man.
Enter MARCIUS

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text: Come I too late?

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
Ay, if you come not in the blood of others,
But mantled in your own.

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
Ay, if you come not in the blood of others,
But mantled in your own.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
O, let me clip ye
In arms as sound as when I woo'd, in heart
As merry as when our nuptial day was done,
And tapers burn'd to bedward!

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
O, let me clip ye
In arms as sound as when I woo'd, in heart
As merry as when our nuptial day was done,
And tapers burn'd to bedward!

O, let me clip ye
In arms as sound as when I woo'd, in heart

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
Flower of warriors,
How is it with Titus Lartius?

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
MARCIUS
COMINIUS
sentences.text:
Where is that slave
Which told me they had beat you to your trenches?
Where is he? call him hither.

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
Where is that slave
Which told me they had beat you to your trenches?
Where is he? call him hither.

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
Where is that slave
Which told me they had beat you to your trenches?
Where is he? call him hither.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Let him alone;
He did inform the truth: but for our gentlemen,
The common file—a plague! tribunes for them!—
The mouse ne'er shunn'd the cat as they did budge
From rascals worse than they.

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text: But how prevail'd you?

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Will the time serve to tell? I do not think.
Where is the enemy? are you lords o' the field?
If not, why cease you till you are so?

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Will the time serve to tell? I do not think.
Where is the enemy? are you lords o' the field?
If not, why cease you till you are so?

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Will the time serve to tell? I do not think.

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
Marcius,
We have at disadvantage fought and did
Retire to win our purpose.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
How lies their battle? know you on which side
They have placed their men of trust?

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
How lies their battle? know you on which side
They have placed their men of trust?

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
As I guess, Marcius,
Their bands i' the vaward are the Antiates,
Of their best trust; o'er them Aufidius,
Their very heart of hope.

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
As I guess, Marcius,
Their bands i' the vaward are the Antiates,
Of their best trust; o'er them Aufidius,
Their very heart of hope.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
I do beseech you,
By all the battles wherein we have fought,
By the blood we have shed together, by the vows
We have made to endure friends, that you directly
Set me against Aufidius and his Antiates;
And that you not delay the present, but,
Filling the air with swords advanced and darts,
We prove this very hour.

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
Though I could wish
You were conducted to a gentle bath
And balms applied to, you, yet dare I never
Deny your asking: take your choice of those
That best can aid your action.

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
Though I could wish
You were conducted to a gentle bath
And balms applied to, you, yet dare I never
Deny your asking: take your choice of those
That best can aid your action.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
Those are they
That most are willing. If any such be here—
As it were sin to doubt— that love this painting
Wherein you see me smear'd; if any fear
Lesser his person than an ill report;
If any think brave death outweighs bad life
And that his country's dearer than himself;
Let him alone, or so many so minded,
Wave thus, to express his disposition,
And follow Marcius.

sentences.speaker: MARCIUS
sentences.text:
O, me alone! make you a sword of me?
If these shows be not outward, which of you
But is four Volsces? none of you but is
Able to bear against the great Aufidius
A shield as hard as his. A certain number,
Though thanks to all, must I select
from all: the rest
Shall bear the business in some other fight,
As cause will be obey'd. Please you to march;
And four shall quickly draw out my command,
Which men are best inclined.

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
March on, my fellows:
Make good this ostentation, and you shall
Divide in all with us.

sentences.speaker: COMINIUS
sentences.text:
March on, my fellows:
Make good this ostentation, and you shall
Divide in all with us.
Exeunt
TITUS LARTIUS, having set a guard upon Corioli, going with drum and trumpet toward COMINIUS and CAIUS MARCIUS, enters with Lieutenant, other Soldiers, and a Scout
TITUS LARTIUS, having set a guard upon Corioli, going with drum and trumpet toward COMINIUS and CAIUS MARCIUS, enters with Lieutenant, other Soldiers, and a Scout

sentences.speaker: LARTIUS
sentences.text:
So, let the ports be guarded: keep your duties,
As I have set them down. If I do send, dispatch
Those centuries to our aid: the rest will serve
For a short holding: if we lose the field,
We cannot keep the town.
